X-rays often reveal stippled calcifications, more common in the patella and Y-cartilage of the hip ( Williams et al., 1972 ). Neuroimaging reveals perisylvian polymicrogyria, delayed myelination, germinolytic cysts, ventricular dilatation ( Poll-The and Gärtner, 2012 ) and cerebellar hypoplasia in some. A lipid peak on brain MRS was found in the white matter in 4 of 5 patients ( Rosewich et al., 2016 ). Patients with the classic form of RCDP type 1 present with shortening of the humeri and, to a lesser extent, the femora, stippling of the epiphyses, coronal clefts of the vertebral bodies, joint contractures, and congenital cataracts. Growth parameters are at the lower limit of normal at birth, but progress to profound growth deficiency postnatally ( Braverman et al., 1993 ). Congenital cardiac anomalies are seen in 40%–60% of patients ( Huffnagel et al., 2013 ; Duker et al., 2016 ). Cervical spinal stenosis and cord compression are common ( Abousamra et al., 2017 ). Seizures are seen in 84% of patients, and all have severe intellectual disability; in one series, no patient was ever able to lift their head when prone, sit without support, stand with limited support, feed themselves, or achieve toilet training ( White et al., 2003 ). Genes PBD Zellweger spectrum is caused by mutations in one of 12 different peroxins necessary for the formation, proliferation, growth, or assembly of peroxisomes. PEX1 accounts for 58% of cases, PEX6 for 16%, and PEX12 for 9%, while the rest are caused by mutations in PEX2 , PEX3 , PEX5 , PEXZ10 , PEX13 , PEX14 , PEX16 , PEX19 , and PEX26 ( Ebberink et al., 2011 ). Enzymes are targeted to the peroxisomes by the presence of a peroxisomal targeting signal (PTS), of which there are two, PTS1 and PTS2. Most peroxisomal proteins contain the PTS1 sequence, while only three enzymes contain the PTS2 sequence: alkylglycerone phosphate synthase, phytanoyl-CoA hydroxylase, and 3-ketoacylthiolase ( Wanders and Waterham, 2006 ). RCDP type 1 is caused by mutations in PEX7 , the PTS2 receptor ( Braverman et al., 1997 ), while a novel type of RCDP is caused by mutations resulting in the loss of only the long isoform of PEX5 ( Barøy et al., 2015 ). PEX5 encodes a long and a short isoform; while both isoforms recognize proteins that contain PTS1, only the long isoform acts as a PTS2 coreceptor. Biochemical defects and pathophysiology Some of the functions of the peroxisomes include the β-oxidation of straight very long-chain fatty acids and of a branched-chain fatty acid (i.e., pristanic acid), the β-oxidation of dicarboxylic fatty acids, α-oxidation of branched-chain fatty acids (i.e., phytanic acid), synthesis of plasmalogens and bile acid, and the catabolism of lysine. Patients with PBD Zellweger spectrum have abnormalities in all of these functions, and thus have increased concentrations of very long-chain fatty acids, dicarboxylic acids and phytanic acid, decreased concentrations of plasmalogens, and increased concentrations of bile acid intermediates (dihydroxycholestanoic and trihydroxycholestanoic acids) and pipecolic acid (a metabolite in the lysine degradation pathway), respectively. It should be noted that the accumulation of phytanic acid relies upon dietary intake and, since the amount of phytanic acid in breast milk is low, newborns can have normal phytanic acid concentrations. RCDP type 1 is caused by a decreased import of peroxisomal proteins containing the PTS2 sequence, and thus patients only have decreased levels of plasmalogens (given deficient activity of alkylglycerone phosphate synthase) and increased levels of phytanic acid (given deficiency of phytanoyl-CoA hydroxylase); however, the concentrations of very long-chain fatty acids and pipecolic acid are normal. Other forms of RCDP exist, caused by deficiencies in single enzymes involved in the biosynthesis of plasmalogens, such as glyceronephosphate O -acyltransferase (encoded by GNPAT and associated with RCDP type 2) and alkylglycerone phosphate synthase (encoded by AGPS , associated with RCDP type 3). Other single-enzyme deficiencies include d -bifunctional protein (encoded by HSD17B4 and responsible for the second and third steps in β-oxidation of straight very long-chain and branched-chain fatty acids), and of acyl-CoA oxidase (encoded by ACOX1 and required for the first step in the β-oxidation of straight very long-chain fatty acids). d -bifunctional protein deficiency is sometimes known as pseudo-Zellweger syndrome and is accompanied by elevation of very long-chain fatty acids and phytanic acid, while peroxisomal acyl-CoA oxidase deficiency is sometimes known as pseudo-NALD and is associated with elevated levels of very long-chain fatty acids only. Treatment and outcomes Supportive therapies include treatment of seizures, adrenal insufficiency, liposoluble vitamin deficiency, and hearing and vision loss ( Klouwer et al., 2015 ). Supplementation with docosahexanoic acid (DHA) is sometimes recommended, based on its importance for brain and retinal function; however, its benefits remain controversial, as one group reported clinical benefits ( Martínez et al., 2000 ; Noguer and Martinez, 2010 ) while another one did not ( Paker et al., 2010 ). Cholic acid bypasses the block in bile acid synthesis and thus causes feedback inhibition of the bile acid synthetic pathway, leading to a decrease in the toxic bile acid intermediates. It was recently approved for patients with Zellweger spectrum who have liver disease or decreased liposoluble vitamin absorption. It is well tolerated and leads to improvement of liver disease ( Heubi et al., 2017 ), although in a minority of patients with advanced liver disease it might lead to an increase in transaminases and worsening of cholestasis ( Berendse et al., 2016 ). CHOLESTEROL SYNTHESIS DISORDERS Clinical symptoms Mevalonate kinase deficiency is a disorder in the presqualene cholesterol biosynthetic pathway, characterized by recurrent inflammatory symptoms such as fever, lymphadenopathy, arthralgias, and abdominal pain, with each episode lasting typically 3–6 days ( Ter Haar et al., 2016 ). Onset in the neonatal period is seen in about one-third of patients ( Bader-Meunier et al., 2011 ). Neurologic symptoms include cerebellar ataxia in 3% of patients, seizures in 5%, and intellectual disability in 2%–4% ( Zhang, 2016 ), although they are more common in the more severe form of the disease, known as mevalonic aciduria. Urine organic acid analysis reveals increased excretion of mevalonic acid, although this finding has a sensitivity of 92%; therefore absence of this finding does not exclude the disease ( Jeyaratnam et al., 2016 ). The prototypical disorder of the postsqualene cholesterol biosynthetic pathway is Smith–Lemli–Opitz syndrome, a condition with multiple congenital anomalies and intellectual disability. Typical physical features include growth retardation, Y-shaped 2–3 toe syndactyly, hypospadias in 50% of affected males, cataracts in 20% of patients, and facial dysmorphic features including bitemporal narrowing, ptosis, anteverted nares, micrognathia, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, and a narrow high-arched palate, or even a cleft palate in 40%–50% of patients ( Nowaczyk and Irons, 2012 ). Microcephaly is seen in 80%–84% of patients, while developmental delay and hypotonia are found in the vast majority. A characteristic behavior is that of opistokinesis, or sudden, rapid retropulsion of the head or torso. Neuroimaging reveals median or paramedian structural anomalies, such as abnormalities of the septum pellucidum in 76% of patients and abnormalities of the corpus callosum in 69% ( Lee et al., 2013 ). A lipid peak was found on brain MRS in 2 of 16 patients ( Caruso et al., 2004 ). The total cholesterol level is decreased in around 90% of patients, while 7-dehydrocholesterol and 8-dehydrocholesterol levels are increased. Low maternal estriol on second-trimester serum screening is a characteristic prenatal finding ( Shinawi et al., 2005 ). Genes Mevalonate kinase deficiency is caused by mutations in the MVK gene, while Smith–Lemli–Opitz syndrome is caused by mutations in DHCR7 . Other disorders in the postsqualene pathway that can be accompanied by multiple congenital anomalies and severe neurologic involvement include lathosterolosis, caused by mutations in SC5D , and desmosterolosis, caused by mutations in DHCR24 . Biochemical defects and pathophysiology The inflammatory nature of mevalonate kinase deficiency results from a lack of posttranslational prenylation of certain proteins. In particular, the metabolic block leads to decreased synthesis of isoprenoids, such as farnesylpyrophosphate and geranylgeranylpyrophosphate. The decreased prenylation of Rab GTPases leads to increased release of interleukin 1 beta ( van der Burgh et al., 2014 ; Jurczyluk et al., 2016 ). Smith–Lemli–Opitz syndrome results from a defect in 7-dehydrocholesterol reductase, which converts 7-dehydrocholesterol to cholesterol. This leads to an accumulation of 7-dehydrocholesterol, which gets converted to 8-dehydrocholesterol by the action of 3β-hydroxysteroid-Δ8-Δ7-isomerase. Treatment and outcomes Mevalonate kinase deficiency is treated by antiinflammatory agents such as NSAIDs, corticosteroids, anti-TNF antagonists, and anti-IL1 antagonists; cases that are refractory to the aforementioned therapies might need hematopoietic stem cell transplantation ( Favier and Schulert, 2016 ). Cholesterol supplementation should be considered in every patient, as it has minimal adverse effects and is associated with improved growth and it improves photosensitivity ( Azurdia et al., 2001 ; Starck et al., 2002 ). It does not, however, lead to improved developmental progress ( Sikora et al., 2004 ) and probably does not significantly alter sterol levels in the brain, given the limited capacity for cholesterol to cross the blood–brain barrier, as the brain relies heavily on de novo cholesterol biosynthesis. Simvastatin leads to decreased levels of 7- and 8-dehydrocholesterol, but does not lead to improved growth or behavior ( Haas et al., 2007 ). CONGENITAL DISORDERS OF GLYCOSYLATION Clinical symptoms There are over 100 different types of congenital disorders of glycosylation, or CDGs ( Jaeken and Péanne, 2017 ). The most common type of CDG, arising from phosphomannomutase deficiency, is a multisystemic disorder that can present in the newborn period with hydrops, pleural effusions, pericardial effusions, failure to thrive, hypotonia, microcephaly, developmental delay, strabismus, and roving eye movements ( Grünewald, 2009 ; Freeze et al., 2012 ). Some neurologic complications of the disease include cerebellar atrophy, which can be present as early as the neonatal period, while seizures and stroke-like episodes typically start in early childhood. Peripheral neuropathy develops over time. Important clues toward the diagnosis are the presence of inverted nipples and abnormal fat distribution, including supragluteal and suprapubic fat pads; both findings, however, can disappear over the course of the first few years of life. Other organ systems that can be involved include the gastrointestinal (protein-losing enteropathy, hepatocellular liver disease), hematologic (coagulopathy, thrombosis), endocrine (hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia, hypothyroidism), skeletal (kyphoscoliosis, pectus deformity), ocular (retinitis pigmentosa), renal (tubulopathy, proteinuria, cysts), and cardiac (hypertrophic cardiomyopathy) systems ( Grünewald, 2009 ). Genes The most common type of CDG is caused by a deficiency of phosphomannomutase 2, encoded by PMM2 ( Matthijs et al., 1997 ). Most CDGs are inherited in a recessive manner, but other inheritance patterns such as X-linked are possible, as in ALG13-CDG ( Timal et al., 2012 ), SLC35A2-CDG ( Ng et al., 2013 ), or PIGA-CDG ( Johnston et al., 2012 ). Biochemical defects and pathophysiology It is estimated that half of all proteins are glycosylated ( Apweiler et al., 1999 ) and that about 2% of the whole genome encodes proteins directly involved in glycosylation reactions ( Freeze, 2013 ). In fact, the glycome, or the entire complement of sugars of an organism, is estimated to be several times larger than the proteome ( Freeze, 2006 ). CDGs lead to human disease due to an inability to properly synthesize glycan chains. In particular, there can be a defect in protein N-linked glycosylation (a defect in attaching a glycan chain to the amino group of asparagine) or protein O-linked glycosylation (attaching the glycan to the hydroxyl group of serine or threonine), a defect in glycosphingolipid glycosylation, a defect in the synthesis of glycophosphatidylinositol (GPI) anchors, or a defect in vesicular trafficking. Treatment and outcomes There is no treatment for the most common type of CDG, PMM2-CDG. Some improvement can be seen with administration of mannose to patients with MPI-CDG ( Niehues et al., 1998 ), fucose to patients with SLC35C1-CDG ( Marquardt et al., 1999 ), or butyrate for PIGM-CDG ( Almeida et al., 2007 ). DISORDERS OF COPPER METABOLISM Clinical symptoms The disorders of copper metabolism include Menkes disease, Huppke–Brendel syndrome, and MEDNIK syndrome. The earliest manifestations of Menkes disease typically include hypothermia and prolonged hyperbilirubinemia ( Tümer and Møller, 2010 ). A typical finding is that of sparse, coarse, brittle hair which, upon microscopic examination, represents pili torti. Connective tissue abnormalities include pudgy cheeks and cutis laxa on physical examination, arterial tortuosity on brain MRI, and bladder diverticula on pelvic ultrasound. By approximately 3 months of age, affected boys will start showing frank findings of neurologic involvement, including seizures and loss of developmental skills. The pattern of seizures in Menkes disease can be divided into three stages, with an early stage at around 3 months showing focal clonic status epilepticus, an intermediate stage at about 10 months showing intractable infantile spasms, and a late stage at about 25 months showing multifocal seizures, tonic spasms, and myoclonus ( Bahi-Buisson et al., 2006 ). Progressive neurodegeneration leads to death in the first few years of life. Neuroimaging reveals increased arterial tortuosity in 73% of patients, white matter tumefactive lesions in 27%–56%, focal nontumefactive white matter lesions in 35%, and abnormal myelination in 73% of patients ( Manara et al., 2017a ). Gray matter lesions, specifically basal ganglia abnormalities, are seen in 44%–75%, cerebral atrophy in 81%–88%, cerebellar atrophy in 70%–100%, and subdural collections are seen in about a quarter of patients ( Manara et al., 2017b ). Skeletal radiographs can reveal metaphyseal lesions mimicking the corner fractures or bucket-handle fractures from child abuse. The combination of classic metaphyseal lesions and subdural hematomas can indeed lead to a diagnosis of nonaccidental trauma ( Cronin et al., 2012 ). Laboratory findings include low concentrations of serum copper and ceruloplasmin, although these are unreliable markers during the first several weeks of life, as infants under 6 months of age normally have low serum levels of both markers ( Kaler, 1993 ). Instead, an early diagnosis can be established on biochemical grounds using concentrations of catecholamines, since the partial deficiency of dopamine hydroxylase leads to a characteristic pattern of plasma and cerebrospinal fluid neurochemical abnormalities ( Kaler et al., 1993a , b ). Thus, the ratio of a proximal metabolite in the pathway—the dopamine metabolite dyhydroxyphenylacetic acid, DOPAC—to a distal metabolite—dihydroxyphenylglycol, DHPG—or the ratio of dopamine to norepinephrine represent sensitive and specific markers in the diagnosis of Menkes disease during the neonatal period ( Goldstein et al., 2009 ). Huppke et al. described five patients with intellectual disability, congenital cataracts, nystagmus, hearing loss, hypomyelination, cerebral and cerebellar atrophy, early lethality, and low serum copper and ceruloplasmin levels ( Huppke et al., 2012 ), and this novel metabolic condition is named after them. MEDNIK syndrome is an acronym for Mental retardation, Enteropathy, Deafness, peripheral Neuropathy, Ichthyosis, and Keratodermia. Other findings include liver disease with cholestasis and elevated transaminases, decreased serum copper and ceruloplasmin, increased urine copper and increased liver copper concentration, and a mild increase in plasma very long-chain fatty acid levels ( Martinelli et al., 2013 ). Neuroimaging can reveal bilateral T2-weighted hyperintensities of the basal ganglia. Genes Menkes syndrome is caused by mutations in ATP7A , encoding a copper transporter located in the trans-Golgi network. The basic defect hinders transport of copper out of the enterocytes and into the circulation. Huppke–Brendel syndrome is caused by mutations in the SLC33A1 gene, encoding a transporter of acetyl-CoA into the ER lumen ( Huppke et al., 2012 ). MEDNIK syndrome is caused by mutations in AP1S1 , encoding the σ1A subunit of the adaptor protein complex AP-1 ( Montpetit et al., 2008 ). Biochemical defects and pathophysiology The underlying basis of Menkes disease is the defective transport and incorporation of copper into copper-containing enzymes, such as tyrosinase (the deficiency of which causes hypopigmentation of hair and skin), lysyl oxidase (the deficiency of which results in the connective tissue phenotype, due to defective elastin and collagen cross-linking), cytochrome c oxidase (associated with the hypothermia), and ascorbate oxidase, causative of the skeletal changes. For Huppke–Brendel syndrome, the prevailing hypothesis is that reduced acetylation of ceruloplasmin leads to decreased secretion, which results in low serum copper concentrations, as ceruloplasmin carries 95% of serum copper ( Huppke et al., 2012 ). In MEDNIK syndrome, the normal recycling of copper ATPases between the trans-Golgi network and the plasma membrane is disrupted, as it is dependent on adaptor proteins ( Montpetit et al., 2008 ). This leads to the characteristic phenotype of excess copper in the liver (reminiscent of Wilson disease), but lack of copper in other tissues such as the brain (reminiscent of Menkes disease). Treatment and outcomes In Menkes disease, the early administration of subcutaneous copper histidinate soon after birth can lead to some benefit, particularly if the mutation is associated with some residual ATP7A activity ( Kaler et al., 2008 ). For MEDNIK syndrome, treatment with zinc acetate has been associated with improved cholestasis and intellectual development ( Martinelli et al., 2013 ). GLUT1 DEFICIENCY SYNDROME Clinical symptoms The most common presenting symptom is seizures, reported in 61% of patients. The second most common form of presentation, found in 38% of patients, is eye movement abnormalities, such as eye rolling, eye fluttering, frequent eye blinks, or “opsoclonus” ( Akman et al., 2016 ). Although these eye movements are indeed rapid and multidirectional, as in opsoclonus, they can be distinguished from it because a head movement in the same direction as the eye movement accompanies them, and because they are separated by clear intervals of fixation, 200–800ms in length ( Pearson et al., 2017 ). Eighteen of 133 patients (13.5%) experienced first symptoms during the neonatal period ( Akman et al., 2016 ). The type of seizure is quite variable, being mixed in 68%, generalized tonic–clonic in 53%, absence in 49%, complex partial in 37%, myoclonic in 27%, drop attacks in 26%, tonic in 12%, simple partial in 3%, and spasms in 3% ( Pong et al., 2012 ). About 1.4% of all cases of idiopathic generalized epilepsy are caused by GLUT1 deficiency ( Arsov et al., 2012b ), as are around 12% of cases of early-onset absence epilepsy presenting in patients before 4 years of age ( Arsov et al., 2012a ). The response to typical antiepileptic drugs should not dissuade the clinician from considering this condition, as some patients can remain seizure-free without a ketogenic diet ( Pong et al., 2012 ). Characteristic laboratory features include low CSF glucose concentration (≤10th centile), a low CSF to blood glucose ratio (≤25th centile), and a low CSF lactate concentration (≤10th centile); cut off values for different ages have been established ( Leen et al., 2013 ). Some patients can have spontaneous ketosis as a means of cerebral metabolic compensation ( Chenouard et al., 2015 ). Genes GLUT1 deficiency syndrome is caused by mutations in the SLC2A1 gene. Most cases are the result of de novo heterozygous mutations in the gene and thus have a dominant pattern of inheritance, although rare cases carry biallelic mutations. Recently, a novel neurodevelopmental condition characterized by intellectual disability, epilepsy, and variable neuropsychiatric features was described, caused by biallelic mutations in SLC45A1 , a cerebral glucose transporter expressed in neurons, unlike GLUT1 that is expressed at the blood–brain barrier ( Srour et al., 2017 ). Biochemical defects and pathophysiology GLUT1 deficiency leads to an inability of glucose to cross the blood–brain barrier. Glucose represents the main source of energy for the brain, which consumes about 20% of the whole-body glucose-derived energy—approximately 5.6 mg glucose/100g brain tissue/min ( Mergenthaler et al., 2013 ). Certain mutations lead not only to altered glucose transport with the consequent energy deficiency, but also to cation leak with osmotic instability, associated with periventricular calcifications, cataracts, hemolysis, and pseudohyperkalemia ( Flatt et al., 2011 ; Bawazir et al., 2012 ; Shibata et al., 2017 ). Treatment and outcomes Treatment involves providing an alternative source of fuel for the brain. In particular, ketone bodies cross the blood–brain barrier via a monocarboxylate transporter (MCT1), and thus a ketogenic diet represents the treatment of choice ( Klepper et al., 2004 ). Some patients can still show recurrence of seizures despite adequate ketosis ( Klepper et al., 2005 ), and up to one-third of patients respond poorly to a ketogenic diet ( Pascual et al., 2014 ). Triheptanoin has also been tried for anaplerosis, with encouraging results. RECOMMENDED DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP Signs and symptoms of IEMs can be rather nonspecific in the neonatal period. Newborns can present with feeding difficulty, emesis, dyspnea, lethargy, or frank encephalopathy, hypotonia, and/or seizures. In intoxication-type IEMs, there tends to be an asymptomatic period of at least several hours, since the placenta “dialyzes” the accumulated molecules, and it takes time for this intoxicating substance to accumulate after dietary exposure. IEMs should be considered when faced with unexplained neurologic symptoms, particularly if these happen to be progressive or if they follow an uneventful pregnancy and delivery. They should also be considered in the setting of specific laboratory findings, such as hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, or high-anion gap metabolic acidosis. Fig. 22.3 provides a diagnostic algorithm for the different IEMs that can present with central or peripheral hypotonia in the neonate. Fig. 22.4 provides a diagnostic algorithm for IEMs that can present with encephalopathy and/or seizures. Fig. 22.5 provides a tiered algorithm providing guidance on which tests to obtain in order to reach the aforementioned diagnoses. Interactive online tools exist to facilitate diagnosis, such as http://www.treatable-id.org ( van Karnebeek et al., 2012b ) and http://iembase.org ( Lee et al., 2017b ). NEWBORN SCREENING Newborn population screening, pivotal for preventive health care for IEMs, was introduced in the 1960s for the detection of phenylketonuria. In the mid-1990s, MS/MS allowed for extended newborn screening and was consecutively incorporated into screening programs worldwide. MS/MS enables the diagnosis and treatment of a much larger number of metabolic disorders (fatty acid oxidation disorders, organic acidurias, some urea cycle defects, additional disorders of amino acid metabolism). Usually the bloodspot is taken on days 3–6 to allow for sufficient nutrient load. For the clinician, it is important to realize that great variability exists in the panel of disorders screened for in different countries and states, as there is no consensus on the basic decision criteria for inclusion. Also, false positives and false negatives exist, and thus biochemical confirmation and clinical evaluation are mandatory. A newborn may become sick before newborn screening results are available, as is often the case for maple syrup urine disease or male OTC deficiency. The consequences of positive screening results differ between conditions. Sometimes only a repeat analysis from a second screening card is required. In other cases, immediate initiation of therapeutic measures is necessary and may require referral to a specialized metabolic center. Parents may need to understand that urgent intervention is necessary, even though newborn screening also detects asymptomatic variants of many conditions. FUTURE DIRECTIONS The number of recognized IEMs and insights into their varying phenotypes even during the neonatal period are expanding rapidly, through the use of genomic, metabolomic, and deep phenotyping technologies, such as high-resolution and functional neuroimaging. Prompt recognition of these conditions for treatment and best outcomes is essential but challenging; an awareness of their existence and a structured diagnostic approach supported by digital tools aid the clinician. Understanding disease mechanisms and phenotypic modifiers is currently the big challenge; systems biology approaches and model organism studies pave the way. New therapies are not only under development but are also in clinical trials, and they vary from medical diets to pharmacotherapy and gene therapy. The potential to treat an increasing number of IEMs, however rare, motivates the expansion of newborn screening programs; however, the costs of therapy are sometimes prohibitive, such as enzyme replacement therapy in mucopolysaccharidoses. On the other hand, early identification of diseases that are asymptomatic during the newborn period, such as X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy, allow for the possible scrutiny of the disease course during early childhood years and aid in our pathophysiologic understanding and ability to provide timely interventions. Now, more than ever, in the age of big data, interdisciplinary collaboration is key to providing state-of-the-art care for neonates suffering IEMs. To diagnose and treat, the neonatal neurologist needs input from other clinicians (metabolic specialists, geneticists, radiologists, neonatologists) as well as laboratory specialists (biochemists, molecular geneticists, bioinformaticians) and researchers in the aforementioned fields.

title: The genetic structure of the Turkish population reveals high levels of variation and admixture.
abstract: The construction of population-based variomes has contributed substantially to our understanding of the genetic basis of human inherited disease. Here, we investigated the genetic structure of Turkey from 3,362 unrelated subjects whose whole exomes (n = 2,589) or whole genomes (n = 773) were sequenced to generate a Turkish (TR) Variome that should serve to facilitate disease gene discovery in Turkey. Consistent with the history of present-day Turkey as a crossroads between Europe and Asia, we found extensive admixture between Balkan, Caucasus, Middle Eastern, and European populations with a closer genetic relationship of the TR population to Europeans than hitherto appreciated. We determined that 50% of TR individuals had high inbreeding coefficients (≥0.0156) with runs of homozygosity longer than 4 Mb being found exclusively in the TR population when compared to 1000 Genomes Project populations. We also found that 28% of exome and 49% of genome variants in the very rare range (allele frequency < 0.005) are unique to the modern TR population. We annotated these variants based on their functional consequences to establish a TR Variome containing alleles of potential medical relevance, a repository of homozygous loss-of-function variants and a TR reference panel for genotype imputation using high-quality haplotypes, to facilitate genome-wide association studies. In addition to providing information on the genetic structure of the modern TR population, these data provide an invaluable resource for future studies to identify variants that are associated with specific phenotypes as well as establishing the phenotypic consequences of mutations in specific genes.

In This Issue 118 36 7 9 2021 eiti3621118 Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America 10.1073/iti3621118 PMC8433537 Correction for Kars et al., The genetic structure of the Turkish population reveals high levels of variation and admixture 118 52 22 12 2021 e2120031118 Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America 10.1073/pnas.2120031118 PMC8719895 34949644 Significance We delineated the fine-scale genetic structure of the Turkish population by using sequencing data of 3,362 unrelated Turkish individuals from different geographical origins and demonstrated the position of Turkey in terms of human migration and genetic drift. The results show that the genetic structure of present-day Anatolia was shaped by historical and modern-day migrations, high levels of admixture, and inbreeding. We observed that modern-day Turkey has close genetic relationships with the neighboring Balkan and Caucasus populations. We generated a Turkish Variome which defines the extent of variation observed in Turkey, listed homozygous loss-of-function variants and clinically relevant variants in the cohort, and generated an imputation panel for future genome-wide association studies. The construction of population-based variomes has contributed substantially to our understanding of the genetic basis of human inherited disease. Here, we investigated the genetic structure of Turkey from 3,362 unrelated subjects whose whole exomes ( n = 2,589) or whole genomes ( n = 773) were sequenced to generate a Turkish (TR) Variome that should serve to facilitate disease gene discovery in Turkey. Consistent with the history of present-day Turkey as a crossroads between Europe and Asia, we found extensive admixture between Balkan, Caucasus, Middle Eastern, and European populations with a closer genetic relationship of the TR population to Europeans than hitherto appreciated. We determined that 50% of TR individuals had high inbreeding coefficients (≥0.0156) with runs of homozygosity longer than 4 Mb being found exclusively in the TR population when compared to 1000 Genomes Project populations. We also found that 28% of exome and 49% of genome variants in the very rare range (allele frequency < 0.005) are unique to the modern TR population. We annotated these variants based on their functional consequences to establish a TR Variome containing alleles of potential medical relevance, a repository of homozygous loss-of-function variants and a TR reference panel for genotype imputation using high-quality haplotypes, to facilitate genome-wide association studies. In addition to providing information on the genetic structure of the modern TR population, these data provide an invaluable resource for future studies to identify variants that are associated with specific phenotypes as well as establishing the phenotypic consequences of mutations in specific genes. title: Clinical exome sequencing findings in 1589 patients.
abstract: Clinical exome sequencing (CES) is important for the diagnosis of Mendelian diseases, which are clinically and etiologically heterogeneous. Sharing of large amounts of CES data associated with clinical findings will increase the accuracy of variant interpretation. We performed a retrospective study to state the diagnostic yield of CES in 1589 patients with a wide phenotypic spectrum. CES was performed using the Sophia Clinical Exome Sequencing Kit with 4493 genes, followed by sequencing on a NextSeq 500 system. The diagnosis rate was 36.8% when only pathogenic and likely pathogenic variants were included. Consanguineous unions and positive family history were associated with a high diagnostic yield. The neurological disease group had the highest number of patients. The groups with high diagnosis rates were ear, eye, and muscle disease groups. Seven candidate genes (EFHC2, HSPB3, FAAH2, ITGB1, GYG2, CD177, and CSTF2T) that are not yet associated with human diseases were identified. Owing to the high diagnostic yield of CES compared with that of other genetic tests, it can be used as a standard diagnostic test in patients with rare genetic disorders that require a wide differential diagnosis, especially in laboratories with limited resources.
full_text: Introduction Inherited Cerebellar Ataxias (ICA) are a group of rare heterogeneous neurodegenerative diseases which share the common pathophysiology of altering the cerebellum and/or its connection with the rest of the nervous system, resulting in gait imbalance, limb incoordination, oculomotor abnormalities, speech impairment, and swallowing problems [ 1 , 2 ]. Their global prevalence of around 1:10,000 makes ICA one of the most emblematic disorder of neurogenetics [ 3 ]. Over the years, more than 200 genes have been associated with ICA, and a further 500 with disorders which include ataxia as one of their symptoms [ 4 , 5 ]. Numerous hurdles stand in the way of reaching a molecular diagnosis in ICA. First, these disorders are well known for their clinical and genetic heterogeneity, meaning that a same phenotype may be associated with numerous genes and that a given gene may be associated with multiple phenotypes. Second, as shown by the various modes of transmission associated with them (autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, X-linked and mitochondrial), a wide variety of altered cellular and molecular mechanisms may generate ICA [ 6 ]. Reaching a molecular diagnosis for a patient with an ICA furthermore requires to be able to reliably detect not only single nucleotide variants (SNVs) and indels, but also copy number variations (CNVs), structural variants (SVs) and most importantly short tandem repeats (STR) [ 7 ]. STR are repeated sequences of (usually) 1 to 6 adjacent nucleotides [ 8 ] such as CAG [ 9 ] or GGCCTG [ 10 ], which cause genome instability with a tendency to expand through replication slippage and defects in DNA repair [ 11 ]. Indeed, in SCAs, DNA repair mechanisms are essential for maintaining the integrity of STR during replication and translation [ 12 ]. A great number of ICA are associated with STR expansions. This includes autosomal dominant forms such as the historical polyglutamine spinocerebellar ataxias (SCA) caused by an exonic CAG repeat expansion [ 13 ], autosomal recessive forms such as Friedreich's ataxia caused by an intronic GAA trinucleotide expansion [ 14 ], and X-linked forms such as the Fragile X-associated tremor ataxia syndrome (FXTAS) secondary to an expansion of 55–200 CGG [ 7 ]. Interestingly, the amount of STR in ICA seem higher than any other neurogenetic diseases [ 15 ] (Table 1 ). This makes detection of STR even more critical in ICA. To date, the most effective methods for detecting STRs remain targeted technologies such as PCR or southern blots, which can only detect one STR at a time and are expensive and time-consuming when used at a large scale [ 16 ]. Since 2010’s Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) techniques have revolutionized research and molecular diagnosis in rare diseases. NGS, also called massive parallel sequencing, is a general term that refers to a powerful technology that allows to sequence thousands to millions of DNA or RNA molecules simultaneously. It encompasses different applications, that will be discussed below, that are part of the second and third generations of sequencing [ 17 ]. The first generation is represented by Sanger sequencing, the second by short-read sequencing, and the third by long-read sequencing [ 18 ]. Table 1 Summary of inherited cerebellar ataxias associated with tandem repeats Disease Gene STR Location Inheritance DRPLA (#125,370) ATN1 (*607,462) CAG Exonic AD SCA1 ( #164,400) ATXN1 (* 601,556) CAG Exonic AD SCA2 (#183,090) ATXN2 (*601,517) CAG Exonic AD SCA3 (#109,150) ATXN3 (*607,047) CAG Exonic AD SCA4 (#600,223) ZFHX3 (*104,155) GGC Exonic AD SCA6 (#183,086) CACNA1A (*601,011) CAG Exonic AD SCA7 (#164,500) ATXN7 (*607,640) CAG Exonic AD SCA8 (#608,768) ATXN8 (*603,680) CTG/CAG 3’UTR AD SCA10 (# 603,516) ATXN10 (*611,150) ATTCT Intronic AD SCA12 (#604,326) PPP2R2B (*604,325) CAG 5’UTR AD SCA17 (# 607,136) TBP (*600,075) CAG Exonic AD SCA27b (#620,174) FGF14 (*601,515) GAA Intronic AD SCA31 (#117,210) BEAN1 (*612,051) TGGAA/TTCCA Intronic AD SCA36 (#614,153) NOP56 (* 614,154) GGCCTG Intronic AD SCA37 (#615,945) DAB1 (*603,448) ATTTC Intronic AD CANVAS (#614,575) RFC1 (*102,579) AAGGG Intronic AR FRDA (#229,300) FXN (*606,829) GAA Intronic AR FXTAS (#300,623) FMR1 (*309,550) CGG 5’UTR XR SCA8 and SCA31 feature bidirectional STR expansions, with repeats occurring on both DNA strands. In SCA8, this results in both a polyglutamine-containing protein (ATXN8) and a non-coding CUG-expansion RNA (ATXN8OS), while in SCA31, the TGGAA/TTCCA pentanucleotide repeats generate abnormal RNAs. STR; short tandem repeat. AD autosomal dominant, AR autosomal recessive, XR X recessive. #MIM phenotype number, *: MIM Gene number In parallel to short-read sequencing, new diagnostic technologies are emerging, which may once again transform the way ICA are diagnosed, offering the possibility to access, at once, all classes of variants (SNVs, indels, SVs,..) including STR and epigenetic variations, in a reliable manner [ 19 , 20 ]. We present here a review on the evolution of diagnostic technologies and discuss their impact on the knowledge of ICA’s molecular bases. We show how one has influenced the other, from historical techniques to the incoming third-generation: long-read sequencing technologies. Past genomic technologies Sanger Sequencing, Southern Blot and Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) were, in the second half of the twentieth century, the three main techniques available to study DNA variations (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1 Past genomic technologies. A: Sanger sequencing. B: Southern Blot. C: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR). Created in BioRender. Lucain, M. (2025) https://BioRender.com/l37l653 /z9ce3xc/30gbjcf Sanger Sequencing, also known as first-generation sequencing, was developed in the second half of the 70’s by Frederick Sanger and represented the first significant step in molecular diagnosis [ 21 , 22 ]. Over the years its principle of irreversible chain termination has been developed into a 5-step process allowing to sequence targeted DNA fragments amplified by specifically engineered primers. These DNA fragments can be of variable sizes (from about 150 to 1200 base pairs) and are then used to progressively delineate the sequence of interest (Fig. 1 ). Sanger sequencing can detect SNVs and indels in the targeted DNA fragment, and offers the advantage to be simple to analyze, quick, and cost-efficient when used for a few analyses [ 21 , 22 ]. Still in the 70’s, Edwin Southern established a technique later referred to as “Southern Blot”: DNA is digested with restriction enzymes generating multiple fragments of different sizes, which undergo gel electrophoresis and are separated by size [ 23 , 24 ] (Fig. 1 ). These fragments are then transferred onto a second membrane on which they can be revealed thanks to specific probes such as a complementary DNA strand marked with radioactive (historically), fluorescent markers, or antibodies. This technique differs from Sanger sequencing as it does not offer the sequence of a DNA of interest, but sorts out molecules of DNA by size, a feature of particular interest to detect STR which essentially consist in longer (and thus bigger) than normal segments of DNAs within a known region [ 19 , 25 , 26 ]. Finally, PCR invented in the early 1980 s by Kary Mullis consists of three steps: a double-stranded DNA molecule is denaturized, primers engineered to target a segment of interest are then linked to it and produce copies of the said segment. Using variation of temperature, the process is repeated in automatic cycles, exponentially producing copies of the target DNA region (Fig. 1 ) [ 27 ]. These techniques have inherent drawbacks which became progressively apparent as time passed. Sanger sequencing has low sensitivity and discovery power for new genes. Discovery of a new gene in human disorder at that time implied linkage analysis, which meant tracking the co-inheritance of disease traits and polymorphic DNA markers (initially RFLPs, later microsatellites) across large family pedigrees; regions showing consistently low recombination with the disease phenotype (high LOD scores) were deemed linked and guided researchers to narrow genomic regions for positional cloning of the underlying genes [ 28 ]. This accounts for the more important discovery of ICA transmitted in an autosomal dominant manner in that era [ 22 , 29 ]. Southern Blot is unsuitable for automatization and requires important amounts of DNA [ 25 ]. As for PCR, it only allows the amplification of a predefined sequences since the primers must match regions of known sequence. Furthermore, the primers used can bind nonspecifically to other sequences similar to the target DNA, generating erroneous interpretations [ 27 , 30 , 31 ]. Finally, these targeted techniques required upstream linkage studies to define a locus of interest. As such, they required the sampling of numerous individuals from large families to identify new genes [ 32 , 33 ]. Present genomic technologies At the beginning of the twenty-first century, second-generation sequencing, revolutionized DNA analysis using a reversible termination technology. Here, the DNA of an individual is conditioned in “libraries” (physical clusters of DNA molecules) and amplified before being sequenced [ 34 , 35 ] (Fig. 2 ). The data generated can then be analyzed via numerous bioinformatic tools (organized in a chosen order called a “pipeline”) to try and detect the variation(s) responsible for an individual’s symptoms [ 36 ]. A typical pipeline includes quality control and trimming [ 37 , 38 ], sequence alignment to a reference genome [ 39 , 40 ], variant calling [ 41 – 43 ] and annotation of the detected variants [ 44 – 46 ]. The final steps involve clinical interpretation based on population databases (such as gnomAD [ 47 ]), variant effect predictors [ 48 – 50 ], and established classification guidelines (e.g., ACMG criteria [ 51 ]). For inherited cerebellar ataxias (ICA), these pipelines must be tailored to capture a wide range of variant types—including SNVs, small indels, copy number variants (CNVs), and structural variants (SVs)—often with the integration of multiple tools. The complexity of ICA genetics makes automated prioritization of variants challenging, highlighting the need for continuous improvement of computational methods and their integration into clinical workflows. It should also be noted that these tools must be adapted to the sequencing technology (i.e., short- or long-read sequencing, as explained below). Fig. 2 Short-read Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS). Created in BioRender. Lucain, M. (2025) https://BioRender.com/2e5ds0i Radical changes differentiate the principles between Sanger sequencing and NGS. While Sanger can sequence some hundreds of base pairs on a given analysis, NGS offers the ability to sequence a trillion [ 52 ]. Furthermore, Sanger sequencing is costly and labor intensive, with estimates of around $500 per Mb. In contrast, sequencing a genome using short-read NGS costed around 600$ in the beginning of 2020 (without taking into consideration bioinformatics and interpretation costs), and prices have only decreased since [ 53 , 54 ]. Second generation sequencing also offers higher sensitivity to detect low-frequency variants, and the possibility to continuously improve the technology as bioinformatic tools are developed or upgraded, making it an altogether highly better sequencing tool [ 52 , 55 , 56 ]. Starting in the early 2010’s, this NGS technology was applied in different forms (Table 2 ). The first one, gene panels (GP), consists in sequencing a limited numbers of genes known to be associated with a certain disorder, here, cerebellar ataxia. When creating a gene panel biologists and clinicians select a list of genes (from half of dozen to several hundreds) to sequence with the idea of reaching a balance between cost and efficiency. The strengths of GP lie in this (possible) cost-efficiency, and in the fact that they tend to be easier to interpret for biologists as they produce less data than other NGS techniques. They also offer a high sequencing depth, synonymous of sensitivity [ 57 , 58 ]. However, this “clinically-guided” approach also constitutes the limitation of GP, especially for highly heterogenous disorders such as ICA in which hundreds of genes may produce a same phenotype. Knowing which gene to include may indeed be particularly tricky, with the risk of ending up with either a comprehensive GP but made of hundreds (if not thousands) of genes, or with a panel of small size but low diagnostic yield. Its use in highly heterogeneous pathologies, where numerous genes are regularly discovered and awaiting discovery, is thus limited. Indeed, this technique prevents the rapid transition from research to diagnosis, as the panel needs to be regularly updated, a lengthy process. It is thus a diagnostic tool to explore known genes but may not explore beyond the genes chosen within the panel. Thus, whenever a new gene is discovered, the patient’s DNA needs to be sequenced again to cover the new gene. Sometimes, patients even need to be resampled if no more DNA is available [ 59 ]. Table 2 comparison of NGS techniques’ features Explored regions Detectable variations Technical characteristics Technic Exons Introns Promotor regions SNV CNV SV Sequenced genes Identified variants Read Depth New gene identification Cost Gene Panel + ± (acc to design) – + + ± 10 1–2 10 2 > 500x – +/+ + Exome sequencing + - (flanking) – + ± ± All 10 4 50–100x (inhomogeneous) + + + Genome sequencing + + + + + + + + + All 1O 6 ~ 30–40x (homogeneous) + + + + SNV single nucleotide variant, CNV copy number variant, SV structural variant. Acc according Another approach called exome sequencing (ES) allows to get rid of such limitations. ES consists in sequencing all the “exons”, which are the coding regions of the genome, representing 1–2% of its entirety [ 60 ]. Unlike GP, ES gives access to all the genes of an individual at once, a feature of high interest in clinically and genetically heterogenous disorders such as ICA. It indeed allows to have access to genes that physicians would not have suspected clinically according to the medical knowledge of the time, and to identify not only variants responsible for known phenotypes in known genes, but also i) variants responsible for new phenotypes in genes known in human pathology, but not in ICA [ 61 ] and ii) variants in genes that are not even known in human pathology [ 62 ]. ES is thus not only a powerful diagnostic tool, but also a marvelous research instrument to increase medical knowledge. However, on a technical point of view, ES does not have a uniform sequencing coverage, does not allow detection of balanced SVs and does not render variants located outside enriched regions (i.e., exons and flanking regions) [ 63 ]. Finally, genome sequencing (GS) consists in sequencing the entirety of the human genome: exons, but also non-coding regions, such as introns, promoters, or regulation regions [ 59 , 64 ]. GS thus theoretically constitutes the ultimate sequencing approach, allowing biologists to access variations all around the genome, offering all the advantages of ES, without its technical weaknesses (Table 2 ). Compared to ES, GS has indeed a better mean coverage [ 65 ], making it more sensitive, and more reliable to detect variations, especially SV [ 66 ]. The limitations of GS are mainly technical: sequencing 3 billion base pairs makes GS the most expensive, and most complicated technique to analyze because of the huge amount of data it generates. NGS technologies (ES, GS) are currently based on a principle of “short-read” sequencing in which the DNA is fragmented during the preparation phase and fragments are sequenced until a size of around 250 bp, a point beyond which sequencing and elongation may mismatch. This fragmentation step presents the drawback of generating a critical loss of information. Consequently, short-read sequencing is not powerful to sequence the G/C rich regions in the genome (or more generally regions of high homology), to detect structural variants, and more importantly, to detect STR expansions (Table 3 ). As short-read sequencing technologies typically generate reads of 75–250 base pairs depending on the platform used [ 67 ], which can be shorter than the full length of expanded STR regions, the expansions cannot be spanned by individual reads, leading to ambiguous or incorrect alignments [ 68 ]. Furthermore, the repetitive nature of STR causes alignment algorithms to misplace reads or collapse them, preventing accurate estimation of repeat number. High GC content and sequence homology further complicate detection in these regions, making STR identification particularly challenging in standard short-read pipelines. All these hurdles make alignment, detection and measuring of STR expansions challenging based on short-read sequencing [ 19 ]. Different techniques have been developed over the years to limit these weaknesses such as PCR-free sequencing [ 69 ]. However, it is the development of bioinformatic programs to detect STR expansions (ExpansionHunter (EH), exSTRa, STRetch, TREDPARSE, or superSTR) that has offered the best answer to detecting STR expansions [ 70 , 71 ]. A comparative overview of such tools is available in Table 4 . Table 3 technical comparison of short- and long-read-based sequencing techniques. STR: short tandem repeat Short-read sequencing Long-read sequencing Read length 100–300 bp 10–30 kb Coverage PCR induces a GC-content-dependent coverage bias. Even in the PCR-free method, a PCR-step is always required in the template amplification The lack of PCR amplification allows for an evenly distributed coverage Telomeres and centromeres can be sequenced Indels > 10 bp Large indels detection is difficult Great precision and recall of indels Structural Variant (SV) SVs in non-repetitive region can be detected with a good efficiency. In contrast, most SVs in STR regions are missed Good detection of SVs in STR and non-repetitive regions Low complexity regions AND Mobile element insertion Low to no detection Good detection of either low complexity variant, such as STR and mobile element insertion Epigenomic study Impossible without further techniques Possible on the same analysis through detection of chemical base modifications and chromatin structure Table 4 Comparative overview of bioinformatic tools for STR expansion detection Tool Input type Detection method Strengths Limitations STR types supported Validation required ExpansionHunter BAM/CRAM Targeted, alignment-based Widely used, fast, compatible with clinical pipelines (e.g., DRAGEN), user-friendly Restricted to known loci; limited sensitivity for novel/unexpected STRs Known pathogenic STR loci Yes (usually by PCR) exSTRa BAM Statistical outlier detection Can detect expansions without prior size thresholds; supports cohort-based analysis Lower performance for single-sample analysis; limited resolution for sizing Known or candidate STR loci Yes STRetch BAM + custom index Genome-wide scan (alignment + outlier) Can detect novel and large expansions genome-wide; customizable reference Requires high-quality coverage; higher computational cost Genome-wide STRs Yes TREDPARSE BAM Local realignment and likelihood modeling Good for targeted loci; can estimate zygosity and repeat size Targeted to trinucleotide repeat diseases; sensitive to coverage variation Targeted (e.g., HD, SCA, FRDA) Yes superSTR FASTQ k-mer-based, reference-free Fast and scalable; does not require alignment; ideal for population-scale screening Limited to detecting very large expansions; not locus-specific by default Genome-wide (motif-based) Yes The input type is the format of NGS data required. Detection method is the algorithm principle of the detection. Validation required indicates whether orthogonal methods (e.g., PCR, southern blot) are needed for confirmation Although these tools have become increasingly reliable over the years, they remain imperfect and still require PCR/Southern blotting to confirm and characterize (size) STR expansions, making STR detection still tedious and challenging to this day [ 72 ]. Future genomic technologies Long-read sequencing The limitations of short-read NGS have led teams to develop other approaches based on the idea of keeping DNA molecules as long as possible. These techniques are gathered under the term “long-read sequencing” (LRS), also known as third-generation sequencing. Several techniques are currently available with 2 standing out: Single Molecule, Real Time (SMRT) and nanopore-based direct electronic sequencing [ 73 , 74 ]. This enables sequencing of 10 kb to several Mb reads with a read accuracy ranging from 87 to 98% [ 36 , 75 ]. Nanopore-based electronic sequencing also allows to detect other characteristics of the DNA strand, such as its methylation level and chromatin accessibility, in term allowing the detection of epigenetic variations [ 76 , 77 ]. Both technologies have their advantages and limitations, with nanopore sequencing allowing better portability, and cost-effectiveness, and SMRT higher per-read accuracy [ 78 ]. The theoretical advantages of LRS are numerous: they allow a better detection of structural variants (SV) including complex rearrangements, but also of variants in regions of high homology, such as STR, homopolymers, or protein-coding genes with pseudogenes [ 79 ] (Table 3 ). Numerous examples have been published over the years, illustrating these theoretical advantages of LRS, not only as a diagnostic tool for heterogenous disorders such as ICA [ 80 , 81 ], but also as a research tool, to identify new genes in neurogenetic disorders [ 82 ]. More specifically, LRS, both SMRT and Nanopore-based electronic sequencing, have shown a reliable capacity to detect the STR expansions associated with ICA [ 10 , 83 , 84 ]. Because the technology underlying LRS is so different from the one used in short-read sequencing, LRS has prompted the development of dedicated bioinformatic tools to harness its full potential [ 20 , 85 ]. For structural variant (SV) detection, tools, such as Sniffles and SVIM, are widely used to identify deletions, duplications, insertions, and complex rearrangements with a sensitivity now higher than the one of short-read methods [ 20 , 79 ]. For STR expansions, tools like Tandem-genotypes, NanoSatellite, and STRique can accurately detect and quantify long STR expansions by analyzing native reads spanning entire repeat regions [ 68 , 71 ]. ONT-specific basecalling and polishing pipelines (e.g., Guppy, Medaka) further improve accuracy, while alignment tools such as Minimap2 are optimized for long and error-prone reads [ 40 ]. Despite their power, these tools face challenges related to read accuracy (especially in homopolymers), limited standardization, and sparse reference databases—particularly for rare neurogenetic diseases like cerebellar ataxias [ 85 ]. Thus, continued algorithm development and benchmarking are essential to fully integrate LRS into clinical diagnostics. LRS also presents other limiting factors: it requires specific, nonstandard DNA extractions to obtain high-molecular-weight DNA and is more expansive than the now widely spread short-read NGS technology, although with time and progress, costs should decrease and become more widely affordable [ 86 ]. RNA sequencing and LRS Beyond DNA sequencing, RNA sequencing techniques should also convert to the growing interest in LRS. RNA sequencing consists in producing and sequencing complementary DNA (cDNA) strands from extracted RNAs [ 87 ]. It is complementary to DNA sequencing as it allows to indirectly detect a pathogenic variant through its consequences on RNA. In a diagnostic approach, it is especially interesting to detect splice variants that may not be identified through ES for instance, or to better characterize their pathogenic effect, the in silico tools to assess them being scarce [ 88 ]. On a more fundamental point of view, it is also a tool to study every part of RNA biology: RNA structure, translation, and above all gene expression, sometimes even at a single-cell scale [ 87 ]. However, when using RNA sequencing one should always keep in mind that gene expression may be tissue dependent and that the most easily available tissue (blood) may not be adequate for some genes in which the protein is not expressed. This constitutes an important limitation for neurological disorders in which access to tissue may be extremely limited. Optical mapping Third generation techniques also include another technology, which, unlike LRS, is not based on sequencing: optical genome mapping. This technology relies on selecting high-molecular-weight DNA molecules and labeling them using probes attached to fluorophores to achieve comprehensive genome labeling. These molecules are then transferred into a chip for scanning. Computer tools are then used to convert the images into digital data to localize the probes and measure the distances between them [ 89 ]. Optical mapping is a very specific tool. Unlike the above-mentioned LRS technologies, it is not a sequencing technique and cannot be used to detect SNV, but only allows the detection of SVs, including CNVs, and STR expansions [ 90 ]. Its application in several neurogenetic diseases suggests that this technique is more reliable and powerful than current technologies for detecting and measuring STR expansions [ 91 ]. Additionally, it enables the analysis of the entire genome—and therefore all STR in a single assay, which is a highly appealing feature for ICA. One of its limitations is that, unlike LRS techniques, optical mapping does not provide information about the sequence of the repeats or whether they are “pure” or interrupted—an element that is critical in many repeat expansion disorders [ 92 , 93 ]. Discussion – From technology to knowledge, and knowledge to technology The world of genomics is rapidly and constantly evolving. A few decades separate first-generation and second-generation sequencing. Only a few years separate second-generation sequencing from third-generation sequencing [ 36 ]. ICA offer a great illustration of the close and reciprocal relationship that may exist between technological advances and medical knowledge. As technology progressed, so did the understanding of ICA’s molecular bases, in turn influencing the development of sequencing and bioinformatic technologies. The first techniques available in the 80 s and 90 s allowed the discovery of the most frequent forms of ICA (polyglutamines SCAs, Friedreich’s ataxia, etc . ) as the power and sensitivity of linkage analyses used at the time required a great number of patients to identify the gene associated with a given ICA [ 56 ]. As an illustration, the GAA trinucleotide expansion of Friedreich’s ataxia was described using a combination of PCR, southern Blot, and “cosmid” plasmid [ 94 ]. Other specific methods were also used to detect expansions of trinucleotides repeats, such as the repeat expansion detection assay. Coupled with PCR, it allowed for the discovery of an untranslated CTG expansion causing SCA8 in 1999 [ 33 ]. Over the years, about 50 ataxias were discovered using these techniques [ 56 ], but they became expensive, time-consuming, and tedious as the numbers of genes associated with ICA increased and the number of analyses required to diagnose a patient with ICA skyrocketed. As a result, the 2000 s saw a slowdown in the discovery of new genes of ataxia, leaving many patients without a molecular diagnosis [ 56 ]. The development and use of NGS technologies in the field of ICA, but more widely in human genetics, led to a revolution rarely seen in the history of medicine. Using these technologies, researchers were able to discover 3 times more new ICA genes than first-generation sequencing tools did, in a third of the time [ 56 ]. ES used alone or in combination with other techniques is considered to be responsible for the discovery of more than 40% of all known ICA genes [ 95 ]. These new tools generated an incredible leap in knowledge on the molecular bases of ICA, on their clinical and genetic overlap with other neurogenetic diseases (especially spastic paraplegia) and opened the way to the blooming era of gene therapies [ 7 , 96 – 99 ]. A first illustration of the impact of this technological improvement on the understanding of ICA is offered by the evolution of classifications over the years. Being very limited by the tools of the time, the first classifications of ICA were based on a mix of clinical and etiological features and were left with a lot of unknown parameters, often including categories of diseases of “unknown etiology” [ 100 ]. Classifications based on anatomopathology were also frequent and gave rise to names of ICA that are still used today (spinocerebellar ataxia, dentatorubal-pallidoluysian atrophy, etc . ) [ 101 ]. The era of NGS motivated a shift in classifications in which ICA’s were sorted out based on their mode of inheritance (autosomal dominant/recessive cerebellar ataxia (ADCA/ARCA), spinocerebellar ataxia recessive (SCAR)) [ 102 , 103 ]. However, these classifications were highly confusing for clinicians, being not comprehensive, partially overlapping, redundant, and eventually poorly clinically relevant [ 1 , 14 ]. Latest international reflections have led to a system based on the idea of associating a gene with its phenotype by adding a prefix “ATX” or “HSP” depending on whether it generates cerebellar ataxia, spastic paraplegia, or both [ 104 ]. However, this classification has limitations, with phenotypic, genetic, and molecular pathway overlaps involved in both ataxias and spastic paraplegias [ 105 ]. One may only expect these classifications to evolve again with the new knowledge third-generation technologies will bring. The fallout of the improvement of sequencing technologies is also seen through the way they transformed the day-to-day diagnosis of ICA patients. Being more practical and easier to implement, GP have been favored by some teams. However, studies using GP have tended to show that they were not an optimal strategy for ICA, offering limited diagnostic yields, and being prone to miss diagnoses because of variants located in unexpected genes [ 15 , 106 ]. GP’s relevance in ICA is indeed limited by the great number of very rare forms/genes of ICA, and by their extreme clinical and genetic heterogeneity, making it difficult to anticipate which gene may include cerebellar ataxia in their phenotype [ 107 , 108 ]. However, ES and its principle of sequencing at once all of an individual’s genes quickly proved to answer GP’s limitations and to be perfectly suited for ICA. Over the years, the diagnostic yield went up to 30–50%, depending on the type of ICA and the cohort studied [ 109 – 113 ]. Countless examples of phenotype expansions made with ES have also shown that knowing the full clinical spectrum of a gene is as important as discovering new ones to reduce the diagnostic gap [ 109 , 114 ]. However, it quickly appeared that many of these genes remained restricted to a few families around the world [ 56 ]. Indeed, while first-generation sequencing techniques required large numbers of patients to detect the gene underlying a given condition, ES allowed the same result with a handful of individuals, leading to discover extremely rare forms of ICA. As a consequence, ES’ diagnostic yields have remained relatively stagnant over the past five years, with recent ES studies reporting results comparable to those from the mid-2010s [ 113 , 115 ]. This was first thought to be the consequence of ES’s technical limitations, limitations that GS is/was expected to offer an answer to (74). The number of studies using GS in ICA cohorts remains scarce [ 116 ], and short-read GS may not have reached its full potential yet. Determining the causality of non-exonic variations remains challenging to this day, and examples of new disorders associated with non-exonic variants are emerging in developmental disorders [ 117 ]. Yet, recent discoveries in the field of ICA are leading the community to reconsider another important limitation of current NGS technologies: its “short-read” base. In 2019 and 2023, two international collaborations identified two genes responsible for frequent forms of late-onset ICA. Critically, both were associated with STR expansions: a biallelic expansion of an intronic repeat in RFC1 (MIM * 102,579) and an intronic GAA repeat expansion in FGF14 (MIM *601,515) [ 118 , 119 ]. The first one provided a major molecular explanation for a syndrome known for years, CANVAS (Cerebellar Ataxia with Neuropathy and Vestibular Areflexia Syndrome) (MIM #614,575), and revealed to be the second most frequent autosomal recessive ataxia as estimations showed that 7/1000 individuals were heterozygous carriers in Western populations [ 7 ]. The second one, SCA27b (#620,174), associated with intronic GAA repeats in the FGF14 gene also redefined perspectives within the neurogenetic community as reproduction studies in Western cohorts showed it was responsible for at least 20% of all autosomal dominant ICA [ 119 – 121 ]. These two entities, caused by nucleotide expansions, highlighted the shortcomings of short-read sequencing technologies for the diagnosis of ICA through their poor ability to detect STR expansions. LRS therefore promises a revolution in the world of ICA, enabling precise detection and quantification of STR expansions [ 79 ], while also detecting SVs, CNVs, but also accessing methylome on a single analysis [ 81 , 122 – 124 ]. Beside variations and STR expansions in exons and introns, LRS may indeed teach us that a significant amount of ICA is associated with epigenetic variations or variations in regulatory elements, or in topological associated domains. Its use, especially in an integrative multiOMICS strategy, may therefore bring a reduction in diagnostic wandering. Proof of principle of the value of LRS in the field of ICA has already been made, regarding not only its ability to reach a diagnosis, but also to identify new genes (and expansions), but examples remain scarce and larger cohort studies are needed [ 119 , 123 , 125 , 126 ]. The relative novelty of LRS also implies that reference databases are still poor or incomplete [ 127 ] and that research is thus needed to complete these databases before LRS may be brought into clinical routine. Yet, the perspective of a significant increase in knowledge in ICA generates excitement as a better insight into their pathophysiology could also lead to the identification of new therapeutic targets, and to new treatments which are still direly needed. Finally, some critical biases deserve to be mentioned when discussing the strength, limitations, and prospects of sequencing techniques: i) all genetic analyses require human interpretation (sometimes aided by artificial intelligence). The importance of the biologist’s expertise should not be underestimated when trying to reach a molecular diagnosis; ii) some individual’s phenotypes are sometimes the sum of more than one genetic disease; iii) the biologist must remain vigilant about reclassified variants as new information becomes available but is not necessarily updated in databases; iv) all technologies have cover biases for complex regions, and even the reference genome GRCh38 is known to lack certain regions such as telomeres; v) databases used to interpret genomic data often present important biases in their representation of population and ethnicities, with a high representation of Caucasian populations, and a poor proportion of population from Africa and Asia. Such biases will need to be addressed in future to harness the full potential of diagnostic technologies in ICA. Supplementary Information Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material. title: Clinical application of next generation sequencing for Mendelian disease diagnosis in the Iranian population.
abstract: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) has been proven to be one of the most powerful diagnostic tools for rare Mendelian disorders. Several studies on the clinical application of NGS in unselected cohorts of Middle Eastern patients have reported a high diagnostic yield of up to 48%, correlated with a high level of consanguinity in these populations. We evaluated the diagnostic utility of NGS-based testing across different clinical indications in 1436 patients from Iran, representing the first study of its kind in this highly consanguineous population. A total of 1075 exome sequencing and 361 targeted gene panel sequencing were performed over 8 years at a single clinical genetics laboratory, with the majority of cases tested as proband-only (91.6%). The overall diagnostic rate was 46.7%, ranging from 24% in patients with an abnormality of prenatal development to over 67% in patients with an abnormality of the skin. We identified 660 pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants, including 241 novel variants, associated with over 342 known genetic conditions. The highly consanguineous nature of this cohort led to the diagnosis of autosomal recessive disorders in the majority of patients (79.1%) and allowed us to determine the shared carrier status of couples for suspected recessive phenotypes in their deceased child(ren) when direct testing was not possible. We also highlight the observations of recessive inheritance of genes previously associated only with dominant disorders and provide an expanded genotype-phenotype spectrum for multiple less-characterized genes. We present the largest mutational spectrum of known Mendelian disease, including possible founder variants, throughout the Iranian population, which can serve as a unique resource for clinical genomic studies locally and beyond.
full_text: Introduction Single-gene Mendelian disorders affect millions of individuals worldwide and account for an important public health burden as predominantly life-threatening or chronically debilitating conditions. Genetic diagnosis can benefit patients and their families by leading to appropriate therapy and providing insights into the prognosis and recurrence risk of the disorder within the family. However, establishing a definitive genetic diagnosis in many cases is a complicated and extended process, and a significant number of patients with presumed genetic disorders remain undiagnosed after receiving conventional clinical evaluation and genetic testing . 1 , 2 In recent years, next-generation sequencing (NGS) has been proven to be one of the most powerful diagnostic tools for rare Mendelian disorders, especially genetically heterogeneous conditions. There are currently two general approaches in the clinical application of NGS assays. When a specific phenotype associated with a number of genes is suspected, targeted gene panel sequencing is applied, whereas exome sequencing (ES) is commonly implemented in the diagnostic evaluation of patients with a wide range of differential diagnoses or uncharacterized genetic diseases. While the current diagnostic rate of NGS-based testing in unselected cohorts of patients generally ranges from 24% to 34% , several studies in Middle Eastern populations have reported a higher yield of up to 48% 3 – 9 . The relatively higher yield in Middle Eastern patients is correlated with a high level of parental consanguinity and the predominance of autosomal recessive (AR) diagnoses in these populations. This signifies the implementation of NGS testing as a primary test of choice in countries within the so-called “consanguinity belt,” including Iran, with a 40% rate of consanguineous marriages 10 – 13 . 14 , 15 Despite the anticipated high frequency of AR disorders in the Iranian population, the focus has been mainly on common monogenic disorders such as thalassemia, cystic fibrosis, phenylketonuria, spinal muscular atrophy, duchene muscular dystrophy, and fragile X syndrome, which are detectable through conventional genetic testing. However, comprehensive data on the prevalence and genetic diversity of rare Mendelian disorders in this region is currently lacking, primarily due to limited access to advanced genome sequencing methods . Consistent with increased affordability and access to NGS technologies in developing countries in recent years, NGS testing is becoming widely ordered by clinicians across Iran. This represented a unique opportunity to evaluate the diagnostic utility of this approach across different clinical indications in Iranian patients and allowed us to create the most comprehensive view of the mutational spectrum of known Mendelian disease throughout the country. We report a retrospective analysis of data from 1436 consecutive cases referred to our clinical diagnostic laboratory for ES or targeted gene panel testing over an 8-year period. 16 Results Patient demographics A total of 1436 Iranian index cases were referred for diagnostic NGS testing, with 1075 (74.9%) submitted for exome sequencing and 361 (25.1%) for targeted gene panel sequencing. NGS was performed on 1315 probands only (solo, 91.6%), 42 probands plus parents (trio, 2.9%), and 79 one or both healthy parents of the deceased affected child(ren) (parents, 5.5%). The majority of patient referrals (62.3%), encompassing 76.4% of ES cases (821/1075), were between 2018 and 2020. The breakdown of clinical indications in ES cases over time is presented in Supplementary Fig. 1 . The patient population comprised 777 males (54.1%) and 614 females (42.8%), in addition to 45 (3.1%) fetal cases from terminated pregnancies. The age of probands at testing, categorized into three age groups, ranged between prenatal to 72 years. Notably, parental consanguinity was present in the majority of cases (72.4%), while most patients (65%) had a negative family history (Table 1 ). Overall, 56% (805/1436) of patients presented with neurological conditions (Supplementary Fig. 2 ).
